entities.description: The complete EETS Extra Series 71 edition in the traditional thirty-two-unit order, with the play text on printed pp. 1-396 and an appendix. Its title page says it was re-edited from the unique manuscript; Pollard's introduction credits England with a new transcript and proof correction against HM 1.
entities.label: George England and Alfred W. Pollard, The Towneley Plays (1897)
notes: Epp finds that the edition was based less on HM 1 than on the 1836 Surtees Society text and identifies reproduced errors, despite the direct-manuscript consultation documented by Pollard.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1897, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff described in U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A; the complete scan is publicly distributed by Internet Archive.
rights.component: 1897 edition text and editorial matter

bibliographic.citation: The Towneley Mysteries, Publications of the Surtees Society 3 (London: J. B. Nichols and Son; William Pickering, 1836), with preface by Joseph Hunter, glossary by James Gordon, James Raine as Society secretary/editor, and manuscript comparison by Joseph Stevenson.
bibliographic.publisher: J. B. Nichols and Son; William Pickering
bibliographic.title: The Towneley Mysteries

entities.description: The first complete printed edition of the manuscript collection. The Surtees preface records Peregrine Edward Towneley's permission to transcribe the manuscript, and the volume says Joseph Stevenson carefully compared the printed sheets with the original.
entities.label: The Towneley Mysteries, Surtees Society (1836)
notes: Epp identifies this as the first complete edition and the substantial base used by the 1897 EETS edition.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1836, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff described in U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A; Internet Archive metadata marks the item not in copyright.

access.provider: The Huntington Library, Art Museum, and Botanical Gardens

dateStatements.label: current Huntington catalog assignment
dateStatements.notes: The catalog also summarizes the assignment as s. XV/XVI.

dateStatements.label: current Epp scholarly argument
dateStatements.notes: This conflicts with the institutional catalog assignment and remains an attributed interpretation.

entities.description: The unique surviving physical witness of this compilation: a 132-folio parchment codex containing thirty-one main collection units and a later Judas fragment. Uniqueness applies to the manuscript compilation, not to every constituent play tradition.

holding.notes: Current citation form: HM 1. Huntington Library, San Marino, CA.

entities.label: Huntington Library, HM 1
notes: The current Huntington catalog records Christopher Towneley's seventeenth-century pressmark, the 1814 sale to John Louis Goldsmid, the 1815 sale to John North, the 1819 return to Peregrine Edward Towneley, the 1883 sale to Bernard Quaritch, sale in 1900 to Sir Edward F. Coates, and acquisition in 1922 by A. S. W. Rosenbach for Henry E. Huntington.
Physical access is restricted, requires curatorial approval, and requires extended retrieval and delivery time.

The current Huntington catalog associates arms on the binding with Sir Isaac Lyon Goldsmid; Epp corrects the ownership/binding history toward John Louis Goldsmid, so the exact heraldic attribution is retained as a conflict.

physicalDescription.extent: 132 folios (ff. 1-132v; f. 132v covered with modern paper)
notes: Twenty-eight leaves are missing, affecting portions of plays 1, 4, 5, 17, 18, 29, and 30 and possibly lost bans and Marian material.
Play 7 is unfinished; plays 7, 8, 31, and 32 were copied out of narrative sequence.
The Judas fragment on ff. 131v-132 was added by a later hand.

rights.basis: The underlying anonymous texts and manuscript compilation long predate modern copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval and early-modern play and fragment texts

next_steps: Perform a systematic three-way collation of HM 1, the 1836 text, and the 1897 text; meanwhile preserve both documented manuscript collation and substantial 1836 version dependence.
openQuestions.question: What proportion of the 1897 EETS text comes directly from HM 1 rather than the 1836 Surtees edition?

next_steps: Inspect a physical or licensed complete 1966 copy and compare title matter, pagination, play text, appendix, notes, glossary, and corrections against the cached 1897 scan.
notes: Searches located bibliographic records and citations but no complete public 1966 page facsimile.
openQuestions.question: What exactly changed between the 1897 edition and the 1966 Oxford/EETS reprint used for the encoding?

next_steps: Keep Huntington's 1490-1510 assignment and Epp's likely-1550s argument separate until updated codicological, paleographical, or material analysis resolves the conflict.
openQuestions.question: How should the conflicting dates for HM 1 be resolved?

next_steps: Periodically recheck Huntington's exact shelfmark search, catalog, ContentDM compound object, IIIF manifest, and institutional announcements; use Aeon reproduction services or the complete 1976 printed facsimile meanwhile.
notes: The current public object is complete as a 60-canvas selected object and partial as a representation of HM 1.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of HM 1 exist outside the audited Huntington selected object?

next_steps: Seek an explicit repository data/XML rights statement; until then distinguish the underlying public-domain texts, current U-M provider assertion, historical OTA wording, and ambiguous repository-license scope.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME software-focused LICENSE cover the XML text, markup, and data, and how should it be reconciled with the embedded OTA restriction and current U-M NoC-US statement?

next_steps: Preserve the current catalog's Sir Isaac Lyon Goldsmid identification and Epp's correction toward John Louis Goldsmid as an attributed conflict; consult heraldic and binding evidence before normalizing.
openQuestions.question: Whose arms appear on the present HM 1 binding?

reviewNotes: The textual, physical, editorial, electronic, and repository layers are modeled separately.
The 1966 reprint is the immediate source named by the XML header. No complete public 1966 carrier was found, so its exact reset/reprint details remain unverified.

The 1897 editors' claim of a new transcript and proof correction against HM 1 is preserved alongside Epp's current finding that the 1836 Surtees text was substantially the base; the relation is therefore modeled as 1836 version dependence plus manuscript collation, not as an uncomplicated direct transcription.

The 1818 Noah/Herod excerpts, Francis Douce's complete Judgment of 1822, and John Payne Collier's Second Shepherds' Play of 1835 were checked through Epp's publication history. They are earlier partial publications, not evidenced intermediaries in the complete 1836-to-1897-to-1966-to-encoding chain.
The 1976 Cawley-Stevens volume is a complete facsimile of the surviving manuscript but no complete public digital copy was found. It is available in physical research-library copies.

The official Huntington ContentDM exact-shelfmark search returned one HM 1 parent object, and its compound object and IIIF Presentation manifest expose exactly 60 selected canvases. Complete coverage of that provider-selected object is distinguished from its partial coverage of HM 1.
No direct physical-source relation is asserted from the surviving York Register to HM 1 because Epp warns that some Towneley readings may predate the register and exact exemplars remain unknown.

lineage.summary: The pinned repository XML derives from a 1993 U-M/Oxford Text Archive encoding of the 1966 Oxford/EETS reprint of George England and Alfred W. Pollard's 1897 edition. Current scholarship finds that the 1897 text substantially depends on the first complete Surtees Society edition of 1836, while its editors also consulted HM 1 and corrected proofs against it; the 1836 edition was transcribed from and checked against the manuscript. Huntington HM 1 is the unique surviving witness of this manuscript compilation. Complete public scans of the 1836 and 1897 editions and a complete public later Epp edition are cached; Huntington's public digital object contains all 60 canvases it publishes but explicitly represents only selected, non-contiguous views of the 132-folio manuscript.

catalogSummary.author: Multiple anonymous playwrights
catalogSummary.date: Constituent plays of varied earlier dates; HM 1 cataloged 1490-1510, while Epp argues likely 1550s; electronic encoding 1993
catalogSummary.editor: George England and Alfred W. Pollard (1897/1966 source edition); University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive encoding (1993)
catalogSummary.region: Northern England; exact origin and the nature of the Wakefield association remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: The Towneley Plays (HM 1 manuscript compilation)

catalogingSubject.label: The Towneley Plays (HM 1 manuscript compilation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous manuscript-specific compilation represented in the pinned U-M/OTA XML and uniquely witnessed by Huntington Library HM 1: thirty-one principal biblical dramatic units plus a later, non-dramatic fragmentary Judas monologue traditionally numbered as item 32. It is not a modern selection, a securely unified sequential-performance cycle, an abstract authorial work, the later Epp edition's reorganized 27-unit sequence plus appendix, the physical manuscript, the 1897/1966 source edition, or the electronic encoding.

contentParts.label: The Creation
contentParts.summary: God creates heaven, angels, the world, and humankind; Lucifer's rebellion and the fall frame the need for redemption.

contentParts.label: The Killing of Abel
notes: The pinned XML has 475 L elements; 473 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Cain and Abel offer sacrifices; Cain murders Abel, is cursed, and resolves to hide from judgment.

contentParts.label: Noah and the Ark
notes: The pinned XML has 569 L elements; 558 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Noah obeys God's command to build the ark while his wife resists; the family survives the Flood and gives thanks.

contentParts.label: Abraham
notes: The pinned XML has 312 L elements; 286 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Abraham prepares to sacrifice Isaac at God's command until an angel stays his hand; the surviving text is incomplete at its end.

contentParts.label: Isaac
notes: The pinned XML has 71 L elements; 70 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: The aged Isaac is deceived into blessing Jacob instead of Esau, and Jacob is sent away for safety.

contentParts.label: Jacob
notes: The pinned XML has 145 L elements; 142 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Jacob journeys toward Mesopotamia, receives divine assurance, and is reconciled with his brother.

contentParts.label: The Prophets
contentParts.summary: A sequence of prophets announces the coming incarnation and salvation; the manuscript unit is unfinished.

contentParts.label: Pharaoh
notes: The pinned XML has 435 L elements; 432 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Moses confronts Pharaoh, the Israelites depart Egypt, and Pharaoh's host is destroyed in the sea.

contentParts.label: Caesar Augustus
contentParts.summary: Caesar's imperial authority and decree for enrollment prepare the narrative setting for Christ's birth.

contentParts.label: The Aunnunciation
contentParts.summary: Gabriel announces Christ's conception to Mary; Joseph's distress and reassurance form the latter portion of the unit.

contentParts.label: The Salutation of Elizabeth
contentParts.summary: Mary visits Elizabeth, who recognizes the blessed pregnancy, and the women exchange praise and blessing.

contentParts.label: The First Shepherds' Play
notes: The pinned XML has 523 L elements; 503 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
The source numbering skips 501; the line total is based on represented logical rows, not a presumed canonical sequence.
contentParts.summary: Shepherds lament hardship, hear the angelic announcement, and visit the Christ child with humble gifts.

contentParts.label: The Second Shepherds' Play
notes: The pinned XML has 768 L elements; 754 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: The Mak sheep-stealing farce gives way to the angelic announcement and the shepherds' visit to the Christ child.

contentParts.label: The Offering of the Magi
contentParts.summary: The Magi follow the star, encounter Herod, offer gifts to Christ, and avoid returning to Herod.

contentParts.label: The Flight Into Egypt
contentParts.summary: Warned by an angel, Joseph leads Mary and the child toward Egypt to escape Herod's violence.

contentParts.label: Herod the Great
notes: The pinned XML has 517 L elements; 513 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Herod responds to the Magi's evasion by ordering the massacre of the Innocents.

contentParts.label: The Purification of Mary
contentParts.summary: Mary presents Jesus at the Temple, where Simeon recognizes the promised child; the unit is incomplete at its end.

contentParts.label: The Play of the Doctors
contentParts.summary: The young Jesus debates learned doctors in the Temple and is reunited with Mary and Joseph; the beginning is missing.

contentParts.label: John the Baptist
contentParts.summary: John preaches repentance, baptizes his hearers, and directs them toward Christ.

contentParts.label: The Conspiracy
notes: The pinned XML has 756 L elements; 755 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Religious authorities conspire against Jesus, Judas bargains to betray him, and Jesus is arrested.

contentParts.label: The Buffeting
notes: The pinned XML has 479 L elements; 450 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Jesus is taken before Annas and Caiaphas, mocked and struck, while Peter's denial unfolds.

contentParts.label: The Scourging
notes: The pinned XML has 417 L elements; 416 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Jesus is examined by Pilate, condemned, scourged, mocked, and led onward to execution.

contentParts.label: The Crucifixion
notes: The pinned XML has 672 L elements; 666 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Soldiers raise Jesus on the cross; his death, lamentation, and the theological meaning of the Passion are dramatized.

contentParts.label: The Talents
contentParts.summary: Soldiers cast lots for Christ's garments and quarrel over their shares after the Crucifixion.

contentParts.label: The Deliverance of Souls
contentParts.summary: Christ descends to hell, defeats its powers, and releases Adam and the other righteous souls.

contentParts.label: The Resurrection of the Lord
notes: The pinned XML has 635 L elements; 634 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: Guards confront the empty tomb, Christ rises, and the news and appearances of the Resurrection spread.

contentParts.label: The Pilgrims
contentParts.summary: Two disciples encounter the risen Christ on the road to Emmaus and recognize him in fellowship.

contentParts.label: Thomas of India
contentParts.summary: Thomas doubts and then touches Christ's wounds before receiving instruction for his apostolic mission.

contentParts.label: The Lord's Ascension
contentParts.summary: Christ commissions the disciples and ascends; the surviving unit breaks off before its original ending.

contentParts.label: The Judgement
notes: The pinned XML has 628 L elements; 622 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
contentParts.summary: At the Last Judgment, the dead rise, accusations are heard, and the saved and damned receive their destinies; its beginning is lost.

contentParts.label: Lazarus
contentParts.summary: Jesus comes to Bethany, consoles Martha and Mary, and raises Lazarus from the dead.

contentParts.label: The Hanging of Judas
notes: Traditionally numbered as play 32, this is treated by Epp as a later appendix rather than a dramatic play by the main scribe.
contentParts.summary: A later, non-dramatic and fragmentary Judas monologue recounts accumulated sins and betrayal before suicide; its ending is missing.

dateStatements.display: Varied earlier dates; most constituent plays predate the surviving manuscript
notes: No single composition date is normalized across works of different origin and revision history.

scope.description: The thirty-one principal play texts rather than the later Judas addition or the date of their compilation

assertion.notes: This is an attributed catalog assertion and conflicts with Epp's later dating.

scope.description: Institutional catalog dating of the surviving HM 1 compilation

assertion.notes: Epp argues from script, initials, and religious-historical context; the exact date remains unresolved.

scope.description: Current scholarly argument for production of the main manuscript compilation

extent.description: Thirty-two top-level verse units in the pinned XML: thirty-one principal dramatic units plus a later fragmentary Judas monologue. The 12,293 logical row count recombines continuation fragments from 12,421 raw L elements. It is an encoding-relative representation metric, not a claim that Epp's alternative 14,385-line editorial lineation is wrong.

assertion.notes: The 2018 Epp edition has a different editorial lineation of 14,385 lines; neither count is silently treated as universally canonical.

formStatements.description: All thirty-two top-level XML units are represented in verse. The logical count of 12,293 rows recombines 117 final and 11 medial continuation fragments rather than treating every L element as a separate intellectual line.

genres.label: Manuscript compilation
notes: Primary whole-scope genre for a disparate manuscript collection; it avoids forcing the units into a coherent performance cycle.

scope.description: Intermittent rubrics, invocations, liturgical phrases, and brief formulae embedded across the compilation rather than sustained Latin text

notes: Content parts follow the exact traditional/XML order; Epp's later reordering and recombination are editorial analysis, not a replacement cataloging hierarchy.
The 31 principal play units and the later Judas fragment are counted separately so that traditional item number 32 remains discoverable without misclassifying it as a main-scribe dramatic play.
Shared printed boundary pages are intentional: adjacent units may begin and end on the same 1897 page.
The Huntington catalog's 'unique manuscript' claim is interpreted as uniqueness of this compilation, not as proof that every constituent play lacks parallel versions.
The XML header's unqualified 1460 creation date is retained as a legacy source assertion only; it is not normalized as the date of either the heterogeneous constituent plays or HM 1.
No place-of-composition statement is made for Wakefield, and no normalized compilation date is preferred across the institutional and scholarly conflict.

next_steps: Preserve the Huntington 1490-1510 catalog assignment and Epp's likely-1550s argument as attributed alternatives; seek updated codicological or paleographical analysis before normalizing one date.
openQuestions.question: When was the main HM 1 compilation produced?

next_steps: Retain Wakefield as an association rather than a composition or performance fact; review new documentary, linguistic, and codicological scholarship before changing the compilation model.
notes: Current evidence does not support treating all units as a coherent guild cycle for sequential performance; Epp suggests devotional reading under Mary Tudor as a possibility, not a proven purpose.
openQuestions.question: What is the compilation's precise relationship to Wakefield, performance, and devotional use?

next_steps: Keep York relationships part-scoped and non-physical until textual scholarship identifies specific exemplars; do not equate the surviving York Register with a proven direct source.
openQuestions.question: Which York-tradition exemplars underlie the part-specific borrowings?

next_steps: Preserve both the 1897 editors' new-transcript/proof-correction claim and Epp's finding that 1836 was substantially the base; a systematic variant audit would be needed to quantify dependence.
openQuestions.question: How much of the 1897 EETS text derives directly from HM 1 rather than the 1836 Surtees edition?

next_steps: Inspect a physical or licensed complete copy of the 1966 reprint and compare title matter, pagination, text, appendix, and corrections against the cached 1897 volume.
openQuestions.question: Did the 1966 OUP reprint make any substantive or paratextual changes to the 1897 EETS edition?

next_steps: Recheck the Huntington catalog, exact shelfmark search, compound object, and IIIF manifest; meanwhile use the public 60-canvas selected object, request the restricted manuscript, order reproductions through Aeon, or consult the complete 1976 printed facsimile onsite.
notes: The official Huntington object explicitly says the manuscript was only partially scanned and publishes 60 non-contiguous canvases.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of all surviving HM 1 leaves available?

notes: This broad association does not flatten the constituent plays' varied origins.

assertion.notes: Wakefield appears on the first extant page and with Noah, and Cain and Abel contains a local allusion, but the collection's precise origin, purpose, and performance relationship remain unknown.

notes: Part-level borrowing, adaptation, and revision complicate any simple one-author account.

scope.description: The thirty-one principal dramatic units, which are of varied origins and do not establish one author
Assembly and shaping of the manuscript collection rather than authorship of every constituent play

assertion.notes: The label remains useful for discussing a stylistic grouping but does not identify a known person; Cain and Abel is sometimes added on other grounds and is not silently included here.

scope.description: Five plays conventionally associated with the distinctive thirteen-line stanza and the disputed Wakefield Master
Later fragmentary Judas monologue added by a hand other than the principal scribe

subjects.label: Passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus

summaries.value: The Towneley Plays preserve an anonymous, manuscript-specific assemblage of Middle English biblical drama moving broadly from Creation through Judgment, with comic shepherd material, Nativity and Passion sequences, Resurrection appearances, and related episodes. The collection is heterogeneous: constituent plays have varied origins and production requirements, several draw on the York dramatic tradition, and current scholarship no longer treats the manuscript as secure evidence for a single sequential Wakefield guild cycle. A later fragmentary Judas monologue, copied by another hand, is traditionally counted as item 32.

The pinned encoding has thirty-two top-level DIV1 units: thirty-one principal dramatic units and The Hanging of Judas. Its order follows the traditional manuscript/1897 sequence. Epp's later edition reorganizes that material as twenty-seven numbered units plus a Judas appendix, combining Isaac with Jacob, reconstructing an Advent sequence, separating Annunciation from Joseph's Trouble, moving Lazarus, and making other editorial changes; that later arrangement is documented but does not replace the corpus boundary.

This record describes the complete intellectual/content boundary encoded in Towneley.xml, not every play ever associated with Wakefield, every lacuna conjectured for HM 1, the physical codex as an object, or later editorial reconstructions. The surviving HM 1 is the unique manuscript witness of this compilation, but some constituent plays have parallels or antecedents in other traditions, especially York; uniqueness of the compilation is not uniqueness of every episode or version.

The XML has 32 DIV1, 32 HEAD, 12,421 L, and 396 PB elements. Recombining 106 initial, 117 final, and 11 medial PART fragments produces 12,293 represented logical verse-line rows; sixteen standalone unnumbered L rows and the First Shepherds' numbering gap are retained rather than normalized away. Epp's current edition totals 14,385 lines because it makes different editorial decisions about internal rhyme and structure. Content structure is complete relative to the pinned XML even though HM 1 itself is materially lacunose.

notes: The qualifier makes the manuscript-compilation boundary explicit and avoids treating all units as a securely established coherent Wakefield performance cycle.

assertion.notes: Retained only as a historically influential label; current evidence does not securely establish its implied unity or performance attribution.

notes: Do not use this label as evidence that the compilation was performed sequentially by Wakefield guilds.
Exact current Huntington catalog label; its word 'cycle' is not adopted here as a structural conclusion.

work.summary: The Towneley Plays preserve an anonymous, manuscript-specific assemblage of Middle English biblical drama moving broadly from Creation through Judgment, with comic shepherd material, Nativity and Passion sequences, Resurrection appearances, and related episodes. The collection is heterogeneous: constituent plays have varied origins and production requirements, several draw on the York dramatic tradition, and current scholarship no longer treats the manuscript as secure evidence for a single sequential Wakefield guild cycle. A later fragmentary Judas monologue, copied by another hand, is traditionally counted as item 32.
